title: Wireless Networking: PHY On-Ramp to RF

lessons.title: What Wireless Is: Data as Radio Waves Through Shared Air
lessons.content:
You use Wi-Fi and a phone every day, but what is actually happening? You already saw how a wired network moves data: tiny electrical signals travel down a copper cable from one machine to another. **Wireless networking sends the exact same data, but as radio waves through the air instead of as electricity through a wire.** A *radio wave* is an invisible ripple of energy that travels through open space at the speed of light. Your device turns the 1s and 0s of your data into a pattern in that wave; the device on the other end reads the pattern back into the same 1s and 0s.

Sending through the air instead of a wire brings three new challenges a wire never had. First, the air is **shared**: a wire is private (only the two ends connected to it hear anything), but radio waves spread out, so every device nearby picks them up. Like a crowded room where people must take turns speaking so they do not talk over each other, wireless devices must **take turns** using the air. Second, signals get **weaker with distance and obstacles**: a wire delivers the same strength end to end, but a radio wave spreads out and fades as it travels, and walls or floors soak up even more of it. That is why Wi-Fi is strong next to the box and weak two rooms away. Third, there is **interference**: other things using the same air, like your microwave oven or a neighbor's Wi-Fi, add noise that can drown out your signal.

There are two big kinds of wireless. **Wi-Fi** is wireless for a *local* area, your home or office, usually reaching across a building. **Cellular** (the 4G/5G your phone uses) is wireless run by phone companies (carriers) with towers that blanket whole cities, so your phone works almost anywhere you go.

Recap: wireless carries the same data as a cable but as radio waves through shared air, which means taking turns, fading with distance and walls, and coping with interference. Wi-Fi covers a building; cellular covers a city.

items.prompt: What does a wireless network actually send through the air?
choices: Electricity flowing through invisible wires
The same data as a cable, carried as radio waves
Light beams you can see in a dark room
Completely different data than a wired network uses
items.explanation: Wireless carries the very same 1s and 0s a cable would; it just rides on radio waves through the air instead of electricity through copper. There are no invisible wires, and the radio waves are not visible light.

items.prompt: A wire is described as 'private' while the air is 'shared'. What does that mean?
choices: A wire costs money and the air is free
Only the two ends of a wire hear its signal, but radio waves spread out so every nearby device picks them up
Wires can be locked and the air cannot
The air carries data faster than any wire
items.explanation: A cable's signal stays inside it, reaching only the device at the other end. Radio waves fan out into the open air, so every device in range receives them. That sharing is the root reason wireless devices must take turns.

items.prompt: Because many devices share the same air, what must wireless devices do that wired devices on their own cable do not?
choices: Take turns sending so they do not talk over each other
Use longer cables
Send data more slowly on purpose at all times
Encrypt every message twice
items.explanation: Shared air is like a crowded room: if two devices transmit at the same moment their signals collide and garble. So they take turns. A device on its own private wire has the wire to itself and need not wait.

items.prompt: Your Wi-Fi is strong next to the box but weak two rooms away. Why?
choices: The data runs out the farther it travels
Radio signals spread out and fade with distance, and walls absorb even more of them
Wireless only works in the same room as the box
The box turns itself down at night
items.explanation: A radio wave weakens as it spreads through the air, and solid objects like walls and floors soak up part of it. A cable, by contrast, delivers the same strength from end to end. Distance plus obstacles is why far-away Wi-Fi is weak.

items.prompt: Your microwave oven and a neighbor's Wi-Fi can make your own Wi-Fi worse. What is this called?
choices: Encryption
Interference
Compression
Buffering
items.explanation: Interference is unwanted signal or noise from other things using the same air; it can drown out or muddy your signal. It is a wireless-specific headache because everyone shares the airwaves. A private wire does not face a neighbor's traffic this way.

items.prompt: Which best describes the difference between Wi-Fi and cellular (4G/5G)?
choices: Wi-Fi is for local areas like a home or office; cellular covers whole cities through carrier towers
Wi-Fi is faster everywhere and cellular is always slower
They are two names for exactly the same thing
Cellular only works indoors and Wi-Fi only works outdoors
items.explanation: Wi-Fi is wireless for a local space (typically one building). Cellular is run by phone companies with towers covering wide areas so a phone works across a city or country. Each suits a different job: local vs wide-area coverage.

items.prompt: A walkie-talkie and a wired desk phone are a useful comparison. Which is more like wireless networking?
choices: The desk phone, because it has a private line to one place
The walkie-talkie, because it sends through shared air that others on the channel can hear
Neither uses signals of any kind
Both are identical in how they reach the other end
items.explanation: A walkie-talkie broadcasts over the air, and anyone on that channel nearby can hear it, just like shared-air wireless. The wired desk phone has a private line, which is more like a wired network connection.

items.prompt: Which list correctly names the three new challenges wireless faces that a plain wire does not?
choices: Shared air (take turns), fading with distance/walls, and interference
Higher cost, heavier cables, and slower computers
Color of the cable, length of the cable, and brand of the router
Too much data, too little data, and wrong data
items.explanation: The three are: the air is shared so devices must take turns; signals weaken over distance and through obstacles; and other sources cause interference. These all come from sending through open air rather than a private wire.

lessons.title: Wi-Fi Basics: Joining a Network, Bands as Lanes, and Taking Turns
lessons.content:
Now that you know wireless sends data as radio waves through shared air, let us look at **Wi-Fi**, the wireless you use at home and work. At the center of any Wi-Fi network is an **access point** (often the same box as your home router). The access point is the hub everyone connects through, like the front desk of a building that all messages pass through.

Every Wi-Fi network broadcasts a name so you can find it. That name is the **SSID** (it just means the network's name, like 'HomeWiFi' or 'CoffeeShop'). When you pick a name from the list and type the password, your device **joins** the network (the technical word is *associates*) and from then on talks through that access point. The password matters for security, which we will get to.

Wi-Fi can use different ranges of airwaves called **bands**. Think of them as separate sets of lanes on a road. The main two are **2.4 GHz** and **5 GHz** (a newer one is **6 GHz**). The plain-language rule: 2.4 GHz reaches farther and goes through walls better but is slower; 5 GHz is faster and carries more data but does not reach as far. (You do not need the physics, just the trade-off.) Each band is further split into **channels**, which are like individual lanes within that road, so nearby networks can use different lanes and step on each other less.

Here is the catch from the shared air: on any one channel, **everyone has to take turns; they cannot all transmit at once** or their signals collide. Wi-Fi handles this with a polite take-turns rule called **CSMA/CA** (carrier sense multiple access with collision avoidance). In plain terms: before sending, a device listens to check the channel is quiet; if it is busy, the device waits a short random time before trying again, so two waiting devices do not jump in at the same instant. It is exactly how a polite group conversation works: listen for a gap, then speak.

Recap: a Wi-Fi network is built around an access point with a name (SSID) you join with a password; it runs on bands (2.4 GHz reaches farther, 5/6 GHz are faster) split into channels (lanes); and because the air is shared, devices take turns using CSMA/CA.

items.prompt: What is an access point in a Wi-Fi network?
choices: A password you type to get online
The central box that devices connect through (often your home router)
The name shown in your Wi-Fi list
A type of radio wave
items.explanation: The access point is the hub of the Wi-Fi network; every joined device sends and receives through it. In a home it is usually built into the same box as the router. The name and the password are separate ideas.

items.prompt: When you see 'HomeWiFi' in your list of available networks, what is that?
choices: The SSID, which is just the network's name
The encryption key
The channel number
The access point's electricity level
items.explanation: SSID is simply the network's broadcast name, used so you can find and pick it. Choosing it and entering the password is how your device joins (associates with) that access point.

items.prompt: What does it mean that your phone 'joins' (associates with) a Wi-Fi network?
choices: It permanently saves all data to the router
It connects to that access point and from then on sends and receives its traffic through it
It becomes the new access point for others
It turns the Wi-Fi band from 2.4 to 5 GHz
items.explanation: Joining (associating) means your device has connected to that specific access point and will route its wireless traffic through it. It does not turn your phone into the access point or change the band by itself.

items.prompt: You want Wi-Fi to reach a bedroom far from the router, even if it is a bit slower. Which band tends to help?
choices: 5 GHz, because higher numbers always reach farther
2.4 GHz, because it reaches farther and goes through walls better
Neither band reaches past one room
6 GHz, because it has the longest reach of all
items.explanation: 2.4 GHz travels farther and passes through walls more easily, at the cost of speed. 5 GHz (and 6 GHz) are faster but fade sooner and are blocked more by walls, so they are weaker far away. This is the core range-vs-speed trade-off.

items.prompt: In plain terms, the 2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz choice is a trade-off between:
choices: Cost versus color
Reach versus speed (2.4 GHz reaches farther; 5 GHz is faster but shorter)
Indoor versus outdoor only
Security versus privacy
items.explanation: The everyday rule: 2.4 GHz gives more reach and better wall penetration but less speed; 5 GHz gives more speed and capacity but less reach. You usually cannot maximize both reach and speed at the same time.

items.prompt: Why are Wi-Fi channels often compared to lanes on a road?
choices: They make the signal travel in a straight line
Each band is split into channels so nearby networks can use different lanes and collide less
Channels carry TV programs
There is only ever one channel for everyone
items.explanation: A band is divided into channels (lanes). Putting neighboring networks on different channels keeps them from stepping on each other as much, reducing interference. There are many channels, not just one.

items.prompt: On a single Wi-Fi channel, why can't all the devices simply transmit whenever they want?
choices: The router charges money per message
They share the same air, so simultaneous signals collide and garble each other
Each device has its own private channel anyway
Transmitting at once would use too much electricity
items.explanation: All devices on a channel share the same airwaves. If two send at the same moment, the signals overlap and become unreadable. That is why Wi-Fi enforces taking turns rather than letting everyone talk at once.

items.prompt: How does CSMA/CA let Wi-Fi devices take turns?
choices: Each device sends as fast as possible and ignores the others
A device listens first; if the channel is busy it waits a short random time, then tries again
The access point shouts over everyone continuously
Devices flip a coin and the loser leaves the network
items.explanation: CSMA/CA means: listen before talking, and if the air is busy, back off for a brief random time so two waiting devices do not jump in together. It is the polite 'listen for a gap, then speak' rule for shared air.

items.prompt: What is the everyday job of your Wi-Fi password (WPA2/WPA3)?
choices: It makes the signal reach farther
It locks the network and scrambles (encrypts) your traffic so outsiders can't join or read it
It speeds up the connection
It picks which channel to use
items.explanation: Wi-Fi security (WPA2, and the newer, stronger WPA3) uses your password to keep strangers off the network and to scramble your data over the air so nearby eavesdroppers can't read it. It does not affect range, speed, or channel choice.

lessons.title: Signal, Range, and Speed: Why Wireless Has Trade-offs
lessons.content:
We have said wireless signals fade with distance and walls. This lesson makes that intuition solid, because it explains nearly every wireless annoyance, plus what 'Wi-Fi 6' and similar labels really mean.

**Signal strength** is just how strong the radio wave is when it reaches your device, the same idea as the bars on your phone. Three things sap it. **Distance**: a radio wave spreads out as it travels, so it gets weaker the farther it goes, like a voice getting fainter across a field. **Obstacles**: walls, floors, furniture, even people absorb some of the wave; thick concrete or metal blocks a lot. **Interference**: noise from other devices on the same airwaves competes with your signal. When the signal gets too weak compared to the noise, your device slows down (to stay reliable) or drops the connection.

This leads to a three-way trade-off you can feel every day: **range, speed, and power**. More **range** usually costs **speed** (the farther or more blocked you are, the slower the link runs to keep data intact). More **speed** usually costs range (faster, data-heavy signals like 5/6 GHz fade sooner). And reaching farther or going faster usually costs **power** (battery): pushing a stronger signal or crunching more data drains a phone quicker. Designers pick a balance for each job, which is why a long-range sensor sips power and sends little, while a video call needs lots of speed and stays close to the access point.

This also decodes the **Wi-Fi version names**. Wi-Fi 5, Wi-Fi 6, Wi-Fi 6E, and Wi-Fi 7 are just newer generations; the simple takeaway is **newer = faster and better in crowded places**, with Wi-Fi 6E and 7 adding use of the new 6 GHz band. You do not need the technical letters, just 'higher number is newer and generally faster.'

Recap: signal strength drops with distance, obstacles, and interference; that forces a range-versus-speed-versus-power balance; and Wi-Fi version numbers simply mean newer and faster.

items.prompt: What does 'signal strength' (the bars on your phone) basically measure?
choices: How much data you have left this month
How strong the radio wave is when it reaches your device
How many devices are on the network
How fast your battery drains
items.explanation: Signal strength is how strong the received radio wave is at your device. Strong signal generally means a faster, more reliable link; weak signal means slowdowns or drops. It is not a measure of your data plan or device count.

items.prompt: Which three things most weaken a wireless signal before it reaches you?
choices: Distance, obstacles (walls), and interference
Cable length, cable color, and cable brand
Password length, SSID name, and channel number
Screen brightness, volume, and storage space
items.explanation: Distance spreads and weakens the wave; walls and other objects absorb part of it; interference from other devices competes with it. Together they decide how strong (and thus how fast/reliable) your link is.

items.prompt: Why does a wireless link often slow down when the signal gets weak?
choices: Weak signals carry more data by design
The device drops to a slower, sturdier setting so the data still arrives correctly
The router punishes far-away devices
Weak signals always disconnect instantly
items.explanation: When signal is weak relative to noise, sending fast becomes error-prone, so the link backs off to a slower, more robust rate that survives the conditions. Slowing down trades speed for reliability rather than dropping outright.

items.prompt: A thick concrete or metal wall hurts Wi-Fi more than a thin drywall partition. Why?
choices: Concrete and metal absorb or block much more of the radio wave
Concrete speeds the signal up too much
Drywall is electrically charged
Walls have no effect on radio waves at all
items.explanation: Denser materials like concrete and metal soak up or reflect far more of the wave than light drywall, leaving less signal on the far side. More and thicker obstacles mean weaker Wi-Fi beyond them.

items.prompt: Wireless designers balance three competing goals. Which set is it?
choices: Range, speed, and power (battery)
Color, weight, and price
Volume, brightness, and storage
Upload, download, and Wi-Fi name
items.explanation: The classic wireless trade-off is range vs speed vs power. Pushing one usually costs another: more range or speed tends to drain more battery, and more speed usually means less range.

items.prompt: A tiny battery sensor needs to last years on one coin cell. Which design choice fits?
choices: Maximum speed and constant transmitting
Long range with very low speed and only occasional, brief transmissions to save power
Always use the 5 GHz band at full power
Stream video continuously
items.explanation: To save battery, such a device sends only small amounts of data, rarely, and accepts a low speed. That fits the trade-off: it trades speed for low power and decent range. Constant high-speed transmitting would drain it fast.

items.prompt: What is the simple takeaway of Wi-Fi version names like Wi-Fi 5, 6, 6E, and 7?
choices: Higher numbers are older
Higher numbers are newer and generally faster, especially in crowded places
They tell you the password length
They are unrelated to speed
items.explanation: The version number rises with each new generation; newer means generally faster and better at handling many devices at once. Wi-Fi 6E and 7 also add the new 6 GHz band. You do not need the technical details to use the rule 'newer = faster.'

items.prompt: For a video call that needs lots of speed, where is the best place to sit relative to the access point?
choices: As far away as possible, behind several walls
Close to the access point with few walls in between, so the signal stays strong and fast
It makes no difference where you sit
Outside the building
items.explanation: High-speed use wants a strong signal, and signal is strongest near the access point with few obstacles. Sitting far away or behind walls weakens the signal, forcing a slower link that can stutter on video.

items.prompt: Why might your Wi-Fi get worse exactly when you run the microwave or when many neighbors are online?
choices: Heat from the microwave melts the signal
Both add interference (extra noise) on the airwaves you share, competing with your signal
Neighbors steal your password automatically
Wi-Fi stops working whenever appliances are on
items.explanation: A microwave and crowded neighbor networks put extra noise on the shared airwaves, which competes with your signal and can slow it down. This is interference, one of the three things that sap wireless performance.

lessons.title: Cellular: Towers, Cells, SIM Cards, and 3G to 5G
lessons.content:
Wi-Fi covers a building, but how does your phone get data on a road trip far from any Wi-Fi? Through **cellular**, the wide-area wireless run by phone companies (carriers). The whole point of cellular is **coverage over huge areas**, so it works almost anywhere.

Cellular gets its name from how it divides the map. Carriers put up many **towers**, and each tower covers a zone called a **cell** (picture a city tiled with overlapping zones). Your phone connects to the nearest tower with a strong signal. Splitting a city into many small cells lets the same airwaves be reused in different places at once, serving millions of phones without everyone fighting over the same air.

Because you move, cellular has a trick Wi-Fi mostly does not need: **handoff**. As you drive out of one cell and into the next, the network smoothly passes your call or data session from one tower to the next without dropping it, like a relay race handing off a baton. That is why a call can keep going on the highway.

How does the network know it is *you* and which carrier you pay? A **SIM card** (the little chip in your phone) holds an identity that tells the network who you are, so the right carrier connects you and bills correctly. Swap the SIM and the phone takes on a different identity or carrier.

Cellular has improved in **generations**, each a big step up: **3G** brought usable mobile data (web, basic video), **4G/LTE** made everything much faster and is the workhorse for streaming and video calls, and **5G** is faster still with **lower latency** (less delay, so things feel more instant). The plain takeaway, like Wi-Fi versions: **higher generation = faster and lower delay.** Each generation still uses the same tower-and-cell idea underneath.

Recap: cellular blankets wide areas with towers, each covering a cell; the same airwaves are reused across cells; handoff keeps you connected as you move; a SIM identifies you to your carrier; and 3G to 4G/LTE to 5G means progressively faster with less delay.

items.prompt: What is the main purpose of cellular networks compared with Wi-Fi?
choices: To cover wide areas (whole cities and beyond) so a phone works almost anywhere
To cover a single room very fast
To replace the password on your home network
To work only when Wi-Fi is also present
items.explanation: Cellular is built for wide-area coverage via carrier towers, so your phone has service across cities and along highways. Wi-Fi, by contrast, covers a local area like one building. They serve different scales.

items.prompt: Why is it called 'cellular'?
choices: Phones are made of living cells
The coverage area is divided into many zones called cells, each served by a tower
It only works inside cell-shaped buildings
Because of the battery cells in the phone
items.explanation: The name comes from tiling an area into many cells, each covered by a tower. Your phone uses the nearest strong tower. This layout lets the network cover large areas and reuse airwaves across different cells.

items.prompt: Splitting a city into many small cells (instead of one giant tower) mainly helps because:
choices: It makes phones lighter
The same airwaves can be reused in different cells at once, serving many more users
It removes the need for any towers
It makes the signal travel faster than light
items.explanation: With many cells, the same frequencies are used again in cells that are far enough apart not to interfere, so the network serves far more phones than a single tower could. More cells means more total capacity.

items.prompt: You are on a call while driving and pass from one tower's area into the next without the call dropping. What is this called?
choices: Buffering
Handoff (the network passes you from one tower to the next)
Encryption
Roaming charges
items.explanation: Handoff is the network smoothly transferring your active call or data session from one tower to the next as you move, like passing a baton in a relay. It keeps the connection alive while you travel.

items.prompt: What does the SIM card in a phone do?
choices: Stores all your photos
Holds an identity that tells the cellular network who you are and which carrier you use
Boosts the Wi-Fi signal
Acts as the phone's battery
items.explanation: The SIM is a small chip carrying your network identity, so the carrier knows it is you, connects you, and bills your account. Swapping the SIM changes which identity/carrier the phone uses. It is not storage or a battery.

items.prompt: What is the simple meaning of cellular generations 3G, 4G/LTE, and 5G?
choices: Newer generations are slower but cheaper
Higher generation means faster data and lower delay (latency)
They are different carriers' brand names only
Each generation works only in one specific country
items.explanation: Each generation is a step up: 3G gave usable mobile data, 4G/LTE made it much faster, and 5G is faster still with lower latency (less delay). The rule mirrors Wi-Fi versions: higher number, faster and snappier.

items.prompt: 'Lower latency' in 5G means what, in plain terms?
choices: The phone uses less battery overnight
Less delay, so responses feel more instant (good for video calls and gaming)
The signal reaches more towers
The data plan is cheaper
items.explanation: Latency is the delay between asking for something and getting a response. Lower latency means snappier, more instant-feeling interactions, which helps real-time uses like calls and games. It is about delay, not battery or price.

items.prompt: Do newer cellular generations throw away the cell-and-tower idea?
choices: Yes, 5G uses no towers at all
No, each generation still uses towers covering cells; they just send data faster with less delay
Yes, 5G connects phones directly to satellites only
No, but only 3G uses towers
items.explanation: The underlying structure stays the same: towers, cells, and handoff. Newer generations improve speed and latency on top of that same layout. They do not abandon towers.

items.prompt: When is your phone likely to use cellular instead of Wi-Fi?
choices: When you are away from any Wi-Fi network you can join, such as out on the road
Only when the battery is full
Only at night
Never; phones always use Wi-Fi
items.explanation: Cellular shines for wide-area coverage, so your phone falls back to it whenever there is no usable Wi-Fi nearby, like traveling. Wi-Fi handles local coverage at home or work. They complement each other.

lessons.title: Short-Range Wireless: Bluetooth, IoT, and Picking the Right One
lessons.content:
Wi-Fi and cellular are not the only wireless. Plenty of everyday gadgets use **short-range wireless** built for connecting things just a few meters apart while sipping power.

The one you know best is **Bluetooth**, the wireless behind your earbuds, wireless keyboard, smartwatch, and car audio. It links two nearby devices directly (a 'pairing') over a short distance, a few meters, and is designed to use very little battery. That short range and low power are the whole point: you do not need a keyboard to reach across town, and you do not want it to drain a battery in a day. Bluetooth deliberately trades range and speed for low power and simplicity, exactly the trade-off from the signal lesson.

This low-power, short-range style is central to **IoT** (the *Internet of Things*: everyday objects like light bulbs, door locks, thermostats, and sensors that connect wirelessly). Many IoT gadgets send only tiny bursts of data once in a while, a temperature reading, 'door opened', so they are built to be cheap and to run a long time on a small battery rather than to be fast. Some use Bluetooth; some use other low-power wireless made for the home; and some sensors out in fields or cities use special long-range, very-low-speed wireless so one battery can last years.

All of this comes back to one idea: **there is no single best wireless; you pick one to match the job.** Need to cover a whole city for a phone? Cellular. Fast internet across a building? Wi-Fi. Connect earbuds a meter away on a tiny battery? Bluetooth. A sensor that sends a trickle of data for years? Long-range low-power wireless. Each one balances range, speed, and power differently, which is the recurring theme of this whole topic.

Recap: Bluetooth is short-range, low-power wireless for nearby devices; IoT gadgets favor low power and small data over speed; and the right wireless is always the one whose range/speed/power balance fits the task.

items.prompt: What is Bluetooth best described as?
choices: Wide-area wireless for covering whole cities
Short-range, low-power wireless for connecting nearby devices like earbuds and keyboards
A type of Wi-Fi password
A wired connection for printers
items.explanation: Bluetooth links nearby devices (a few meters apart) using very little power, which suits earbuds, keyboards, watches, and car audio. It is not built for city-wide coverage and is not a password or a wire.

items.prompt: Why does Bluetooth intentionally have short range and modest speed?
choices: To make devices more expensive
So it can use very little battery power, which suits small portable gadgets
Because long range is illegal
To force you to buy Wi-Fi instead
items.explanation: Short range and low speed let Bluetooth sip power, so earbuds or a keyboard can run a long time on a small battery. It deliberately trades range and speed for low power, the same range/speed/power trade-off seen throughout wireless.

items.prompt: What does 'IoT' (Internet of Things) refer to?
choices: A faster version of cellular
Everyday objects like light bulbs, locks, and sensors that connect wirelessly
A brand of Wi-Fi router
The wired internet backbone
items.explanation: IoT means ordinary physical objects, bulbs, thermostats, door locks, sensors, that connect to networks wirelessly to report data or take commands. It is about connected 'things', not a particular speed tier or router brand.

items.prompt: Many IoT sensors are designed for low power and small data rather than speed. Why?
choices: They stream movies all day
They often send only tiny, occasional readings and must run a long time on a small battery
Speed is impossible on any wireless
They are always plugged into the wall
items.explanation: A sensor that reports a temperature or a 'door opened' event sends very little data and needs to last on a small battery, so it is built to be frugal, not fast. High speed would waste power it does not have.

items.prompt: A sensor in a distant field must send a trickle of data and last years on one battery. Which kind of wireless fits?
choices: High-speed 5 GHz Wi-Fi
Long-range, very-low-speed, low-power wireless
Wired Ethernet
Short-range Bluetooth only
items.explanation: Such jobs use special long-range wireless that sends data very slowly but reaches far on tiny power, so one battery lasts years. Wi-Fi is too short-range and power-hungry, and Bluetooth is too short-range for a distant field.

items.prompt: Which wireless choice best matches a phone that must work across an entire city?
choices: Bluetooth
Cellular
A wired cable
Short-range IoT wireless
items.explanation: Cellular is the wide-area option, with carrier towers covering cities and beyond. Bluetooth and short-range IoT wireless only reach a few meters, and a wire cannot follow a moving phone around town.

items.prompt: What is the big-picture lesson about choosing a wireless technology?
choices: Always pick the fastest one for everything
There is no single best; pick the one whose range, speed, and power balance fits the job
Only Wi-Fi is ever worth using
Wireless never matters; use wires for everything
items.explanation: Each technology balances range, speed, and power differently, so the right choice depends on the task: cellular for wide area, Wi-Fi for fast local internet, Bluetooth for nearby low-power links, long-range low-power wireless for distant sensors.

items.prompt: Connecting wireless earbuds to your phone a meter away on a tiny battery is the classic job for:
choices: Cellular
Bluetooth
Long-range field-sensor wireless
A fiber-optic cable
items.explanation: Earbuds need only short range and very low power, which is exactly what Bluetooth is built for. Cellular and long-range sensor wireless are overkill and power-hungry, and a fiber cable obviously cannot connect wireless earbuds.

items.prompt: How does Bluetooth's design reflect the range-speed-power trade-off from earlier?
choices: It maximizes all three at once
It gives up range and speed in exchange for very low power use
It ignores power entirely to maximize range
It has nothing to do with that trade-off
items.explanation: You cannot maximize range, speed, and power savings together. Bluetooth chooses low power and accepts short range and modest speed, the right balance for small, portable, battery-powered devices.
